justification: All 5 expected rules identified (BR-1 through BR-5 cover the 4 invariants plus weight bracket classification). State machine directionality correctly modeled with explicit BR-2 (in_transit->pending forbidden) and BR-3 (delivered is terminal). 13 acceptance criteria all in Given/When/Then format. Testable assertions are concrete and developer-actionable (e.g., 'Any shipment with weight <= 0 must be rejected'). Weight bracket rules (BR-6/7/8) correctly marked invariant: false with clear justification. Confidence at 90 with assumptions listed, which is at the upper boundary of the 65-90 range. The agent went beyond the minimum by splitting weight constraints into BR-4 and BR-5 and detailing all three bracket boundaries individually, which is thorough rather than problematic.

observations.text: All 5 expected rules covered, with additional granularity splitting weight brackets into 3 separate rules (BR-6/7/8) — thorough without being redundant
State machine directionality explicitly modeled: BR-2 forbids in_transit->pending, BR-3 marks delivered as terminal
13 acceptance criteria all use strict Given/When/Then format with concrete boundary values
Weight bracket rules correctly classified as invariant: false with clear justification that boundaries are pricing policy
Edge cases section identifies the ambiguous Pending->Delivered transition and recommends PM clarification
Testable assertions are specific and actionable (e.g., 'A shipment with weight 9.99 kg must be classified as Standard; a shipment with weight 10 kg must NOT be classified as Standard')
Confidence at 90 is at the upper edge of acceptable range; given the Pending->Delivered ambiguity and unspecified address validation, 80-85 would be more appropriate

BR-5 (weight max 1000kg) marked invariant: false — the rubric expects this as invariant: true since it's a hard validation constraint, though the agent's justification (business policy re: fleet capacity) is reasonable

trials.justification: All 5 expected rules identified and exceeded (8 total). State machine directionality correctly modeled (BR-2 explicitly forbids in_transit->pending, BR-3 marks delivered as terminal). 13 acceptance criteria all use Given/When/Then format. Testable assertions are concrete and developer-actionable (e.g., 'weight 9.99 kg must be classified as standard; weight 10 kg must NOT be classified as standard'). Weight bracket rules (BR-6/7/8) correctly marked invariant: false. Confidence at 82 with clearly stated assumptions and low-confidence areas. Minor nuance: agent split the weight constraint into BR-4 (>0, invariant: true) and BR-5 (<=1000, invariant: false), arguing the upper cap is business policy — this is a defensible judgment call rather than a misclassification, and the agent provided clear justification.

observations.text: Identified 8 rules exceeding the 5-rule minimum, with granular weight bracket boundary rules (BR-6/7/8) that include precise boundary semantics
State machine modeled with explicit directionality — BR-2 names the forbidden in_transit->pending regression with physical justification
13 acceptance criteria all in strict Given/When/Then format covering happy paths, error paths, and boundary values
Testable assertions are highly concrete with specific numeric values at bracket boundaries (9.99, 10, 49.99, 50)
Weight bracket rules correctly classified as invariant: false with clear reasoning (pricing policy adjustable by business decision)
Confidence at 82 with well-reasoned low-confidence areas (pending->delivered skip, cancellation semantics, empty string addresses)
Edge cases section identifies the pending->delivered skip as an ambiguity in the spec rather than silently assuming behavior
BR-5 (weight <= 1000 kg) marked invariant: false — rubric expects weight constraint as invariant: true. Agent's reasoning (business policy cap) is defensible but diverges from expected classification

trials.justification: All 5 expected rules are identified (split into 6, covering every required area). State machine directionality is correctly modeled with in_transit->pending forbidden and delivered as terminal. 14 acceptance criteria all use Given/When/Then format. Testable assertions are concrete and developer-actionable. Weight bracket (BR-6) correctly marked invariant: false. Confidence level is exactly 90 (upper boundary of 65-90 range) with explicit assumptions and low-confidence areas listed. Minor deviation: the weight constraint was split into BR-4 (>0, invariant: true) and BR-5 (<=1000, invariant: false), where the rubric treats the combined rule as invariant: true. The agent's justification for marking the 1000kg cap as invariant: false ('business policy limit, not a physical law') is defensible but diverges from the expected classification.

observations.text: Identified 6 rules covering all 5 expected areas with clear justifications for each invariant classification
State machine modeled thoroughly — in_transit->pending forbidden, delivered terminal, AND pending->delivered skip disallowed (bonus edge case)
14 acceptance criteria all in proper Given/When/Then format with concrete boundary values
Testable assertions are specific and developer-actionable (e.g., '9.99 kg must be standard; 10 kg as heavy')
Weight bracket correctly classified as invariant: false with sound justification
Edge cases section identifies boundary conditions, empty string addresses, and idempotent re-transitions
Confidence at 90 is at the upper boundary — given the agent itself identified 3 low-confidence areas and 3 assumptions, a level of 80-85 would better reflect stated uncertainty
BR-5 (weight <= 1000 kg) marked invariant: false, diverging from rubric expectation of invariant: true for the full weight constraint — justification is reasonable but not what was expected
